content.problemStatement:
We all know that Chef got a box of NNN chocolates (numbered 111 through NNN) as his birthday present. For each valid iii, the iii-th chocolate has a sweetness value WiW_iWiâ€‹. Chef is going to play the following randomised game for fun with his brother:


First, an integer kkk between 111 and NNN (inclusive) is chosen uniformly randomly.
Then, kkk times, Chef chooses a chocolate from the box uniformly randomly and independently (each chocolate may be chosen multiple times). For each iii (1â‰¤iâ‰¤k1 \le i \le k1â‰¤iâ‰¤k), let's denote the sweetness of the iii-th chocolate chosen by Chef by AiA_iAiâ€‹.
Chef's brother does the same thing â€• kkk times, he also chooses a chocolate from the box uniformly randomly and independently from each other or Chef's choices (so each chocolate may still be chosen multiple times). For each iii (1â‰¤iâ‰¤k1 \le i \le k1â‰¤iâ‰¤k), let's denote the sweetness of the iii-th chocolate chosen by Chef's brother by BiB_iBiâ€‹.
The score of the game is âˆ‘i=1kâˆ‘j=1kgcd(Ai,Bj)\sum_{i=1}^k \sum_{j=1}^k \mathrm{gcd}(A_i, B_j)âˆ‘i=1kâ€‹âˆ‘j=1kâ€‹gcd(Aiâ€‹,Bjâ€‹).
Find the expected value of the score. It can be proved that this expected value can be expressed as a fraction PQ\frac{P}{Q}QPâ€‹, where PPP and QQQ are positive integers and QQQ is coprime with 998,244,353998,244,353998,244,353. You should compute Pâ‹…Qâˆ’1P \cdot Q^{-1}Pâ‹…Qâˆ’1 modulo 998,244,353998,244,353998,244,353, where Qâˆ’1Q^{-1}Qâˆ’1 denotes the multiplicative inverse of QQQ modulo 998,244,353998,244,353998,244,353.
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers W1,W2,â€¦,WNW_1, W_2, \ldots, W_NW1â€‹,W2â€‹,â€¦,WNâ€‹.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer Pâ‹…Qâˆ’1P \cdot Q^{-1}Pâ‹…Qâˆ’1 modulo 998,244,353998,244,353998,244,353.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 2:

When k=1k = 1k=1, the score is 12â‹…2=21^2 \cdot 2 = 212â‹…2=2.
When k=2k = 2k=2, the score is 22â‹…2=82^2 \cdot 2 = 822â‹…2=8.
When k=3k = 3k=3, the score is 32â‹…2=183^2 \cdot 2 = 1832â‹…2=18.
When k=4k = 4k=4, the score is 42â‹…2=324^2 \cdot 2 = 3242â‹…2=32.

The expected score is 2+8+18+324=15\frac{2+8+18+32}{4} = 1542+8+18+32â€‹=15.
